title: The Michigan Dogman
intro: The Dogman belongs to northern Michigan campfire culture: a tall figure with canine features and an unsettling howl, tied to logging camps, lonely roads, and deep woods. Treat it as folklore — a mirror for how people describe fear, strangeness, and the north woods after dark.

sections.heading: Early reports and geography
sections.body: Popular accounts often cite an 1887 story from a logging camp along the Manistee River in Wexford County, northwest of Clare County — placing the tale in the same pine-and-river country many residents still know. Whether or not one credits a single sighting, the narrative has traveled along rivers and county lines for more than a century.
sections.lore_callout: The legend holds in some tellings that the creature appears on the seventh year of every decade — a detail that belongs to story, not science.

sections.heading: 1987 and the modern legend
sections.body: Traverse City radio host Steve Cook aired a song about the Dogman as an April Fools' piece in 1987. Listeners called in with their own accounts. The bit became a long-running piece of Michigan popular culture and helped standardize the story for new generations.
sections.lore_callout: That broadcast is a documented cultural moment; it does not prove zoology.

sections.heading: Reading the legend responsibly
sections.body: Some writers connect Dogman stories to Odawa sacred geography along the Manistee or to broader shape-shifter themes. Those connections are interpretive. On unicorn.gives we share the lore as part of regional identity — never as a substitute for wildlife biology or public safety information.

seo_description: Michigan Dogman folklore, northern Michigan legend, and cultural context for Clare County and the Land of the Unicorns.
